mainmatter.p: A deductive system \mathcal {D} is called positively univalent when for any positive A \in \mathcal {D}, the type of positive objects B \in \mathcal {D} equipped with a thunkably invertible thunkable map f:B \vdash A is contractible.

frontmatter.title: The univalence condition for deductive systems

mainmatter.p: A deductive system \mathcal {D} is called negatively univalent when for any negative A \in \mathcal {D}, the type of negative objects B \in \mathcal {D} equipped with a linearly invertible linear map f:B \vdash A is contractible.
A deductive system is called univalent when it is both positively univalent and negatively univalent.
Let f:A \vdash B be a morphism in a deductive system \mathcal {D}. We say that f is (thunkably, linearly) invertible when there exists a (thunkable, linear) inverse to f.
Let f:C \vdash D be a morphism in a deductive system \mathcal {D}.

ol.li: We say that f is linear when for all g:B \vdash C and h:A \vdash B, we have h; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;f \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( h;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;f.
We say that f is thunkable when for all g:D \vdash E and h : E \vdash F, we have f; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;h \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;h.

mainmatter.p: A deductive system \mathcal {D} is given by a type \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } of “objects”, and for every pair A,B \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } a set \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws:

ol.li: cut: for each A,B,C \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } and f \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}}, a morphism f;g \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , C \right ) \mathclose {}}.

identity: for each A \in \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} }, a morphism 1 _{ A } \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , A \right ) \mathclose {}} such that 1 _{ A } ;f = f and f;1 _{ B } for each f \in \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}.

mainmatter.p: Let N \in \mathcal {D} be a negative object in a positively univalent deductive system; then the type of downshift structures on N is a proposition. In this case, we may refer to the property of N having a downshift.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let w_0:N \vdash P_0 and w_1:N \vdash P_1 be two downshift structures on N, whose inverse unwrapping maps are written u_0,u_1 respectively. We wish to identity { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0,w_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} with { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1,w_1 \right ) \mathclose {}}.

All four maps w_0,w_1,u_0,u_1 are thunkable: the wrapping maps are thunkable by definition, and the unwrapping maps are thunkable because they are valued in a negative object. Thus we have a thunkably invertible thunkable map u_1;w_0 : P_1 \vdash P_0; because \mathcal {D} is assumed positively univalent, we may identify the pairs { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0, 1 _{ P_0 } :P_0 \vdash P_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} and { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1, { \mathopen {} \left ( u_1;w_0:P_1 \vdash P_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}}. Taking inverses and precomposing with w_0 on both sides, we have { \mathopen {} \left ( P_0,w_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( P_1,w_1 \right ) \mathclose {}} using the fact that w_0 is thunkable and inverse to u_0.

mainmatter.p: A deductive system is called univalent when it is both positively univalent and negatively univalent.
